output:
# CRUX — Cell-Resolved blame for Unit-targeted eXtension

**Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field (Machine Learning):**
The LLM-Modulo framework (Kambhampati et al., 2024) makes unreliable LLM planners trustworthy by wrapping a generator in a *Generate-Test-Critique* loop with a bank of **sound external critics**. Today that loop consumes critics through a **global, plan-level pass/fail verdict** and reacts to any failure by **regenerating the entire plan**. But failures are not plan-global — they live at specific *(plan-step, constraint)* cells that constructive critics (e.g., VAL) *already localize* and then discard at the interface. **CRUX** promotes that thrown-away verdict into a per-*(step, constraint)* **blame field** and routes on it through two orthogonal knobs: *where* to regenerate and *what* to backprompt. The result is a reusable, soundness-preserving **credit-assignment layer** for any verifier-in-the-loop LLM system.

**Here are the five core questions:**

---

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

In an LLM-Modulo generate-test-critique loop where a bank of critics currently returns a global plan-level pass/fail per critic and the loop responds with undifferentiated full-plan regeneration, can we reduce mean backprompt rounds to a sound plan and improve final solve-rate by estimating a per-(plan-step, constraint) blame field B — distributing blame mass from constructive-critic localizations (VAL precondition/action failures, resource-timeline cells, failing unit-test assertions), with an LLM self-attribution fallback for non-localizing pass/fail critics — and feeding B into two orthogonal, decoupled knobs: (KNOB 1, ROUTE/WHERE) a blame-thresholded regeneration mask that freezes low-blame validated plan segments and regenerates only high-blame cells, and (KNOB 2, BACKPROMPT/WHAT) a blame-weighted allocation of a finite backprompt-token budget that targets the highest-blame (step, constraint) cells — such that current LLM-Modulo behavior is recovered EXACTLY when the blame field is constant over all cells, the regeneration mask freezes nothing, and the backprompt budget is spread uniformly (R1), while achieving a target reduction in mean rounds-to-sound-plan at no worse than ε-non-inferior final solve-rate versus the best full-regeneration LLM-Modulo baseline, and strictly beating a rate/entropy-matched random blame field (PLACEBO) applied with a single consistent shuffle shared across both knobs?

---

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Verifier-in-the-loop LLM systems have become the dominant recipe for making unreliable generators trustworthy: LLM-Modulo (Kambhampati et al., 2024; arXiv:2402.01817) wraps a generator in a bank of sound critics, and the same generate-test-repair pattern underlies code agents, tool-use agents, and constraint planners. But every one of these loops shares a hidden inefficiency: when a critic bank reports failure, the system throws away *where* and *why* it failed and regenerates the whole artifact. CRUX matters because it targets the single most wasteful interface in this entire family — the plan-level pass/fail verdict — and shows that the localization information the critics *already compute* (VAL precondition/action cells, resource-timeline violations, failing unit-test assertions) can be promoted into a routed, per-(step, constraint) blame field instead of being discarded. This reframes a ubiquitous architecture rather than proposing a niche trick.

The first stake is test-time compute and sample efficiency. Iterative repair loops are expensive precisely because each round re-pays for tokens and verification on the parts of the artifact that were *already valid*. By freezing validated low-blame segments (Knob 1) and spending a finite backprompt budget only on the highest-blame cells (Knob 2), CRUX converts a constant per-round cost into a cost that scales with the size of the actual defect. As inference-time verification loops become the default way to extract reliability from frozen models, a credit-assignment layer that cuts rounds and tokens is a direct lever on the economics of deployed agentic systems.

The second stake is a soundness-preserving data flywheel — the fine-tuning motivation in the LLM-Modulo intro itself. The output of a sound critic loop is not just a correct artifact; it is a *critic-verified* training example (correct relative to the critic bank's constraints). Fewer, more targeted repair rounds mean a higher yield of soundness-certified plans per unit of compute, and therefore a cleaner, larger synthetic corpus for fine-tuning the generator. CRUX thus improves both halves of the loop: it makes test-time repair cheaper *and* makes the resulting data corpus richer and cheaper to harvest, closing a self-improvement cycle without sacrificing the soundness guarantee (*the critics'*, inherited — not newly produced — by CRUX) that distinguishes neuro-symbolic loops from pure self-critique.

The third stake is generality. The blame field is defined over (step × constraint) cells, which are *natural* wherever a structured artifact is checked against composable constraints: PDDL planning (action × precondition/goal), code repair (line/function × failing unit test), constraint-rich travel and scheduling (itinerary slot × budget/time/logistics constraint, as in TravelPlanner, Xie et al. 2024, arXiv:2402.01622; Natural Plan, Google 2024, arXiv:2406.04520), and tool-use agents (call × tool-error class). Because CRUX consumes *any* critic that can localize and degrades gracefully to today's behavior when critics cannot (recovery cases R1–R5), it is a reusable credit-assignment substrate that any verifier-in-the-loop system can adopt incrementally, not a single-domain method.

Finally, CRUX is interesting methodologically because it is *honest about its own ablations*. Its recovery ladder makes the baselines exact special cases (R2 is literally per-critic plan-level pass/fail; R5 is byte-for-byte the baseline), and its falsifiers (ORACLE upper bound, entropy-matched PLACEBO blame field, ε-non-inferiority DO-NO-HARM floor) pre-register the ways it could fail. This gives the community a clean experimental template for asking a question that has been latent for two years: *how much of repair-loop cost is wasted because we refuse to localize blame?*

---

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The difficulty is not "ask the LLM where it went wrong" — it is building a *sound, comparable, per-cell* blame signal and acting on it without breaking the plan. Six concrete obstacles:

(a) **Heterogeneous-localization normalization.** The constructive critics speak different languages. VAL emits a failing *action/precondition* at a plan step; a resource/timeline checker emits an overrun on a *resource × timestep* cell; a unit-test runner emits a *failing assertion* tied to a code step. These live in different index spaces with different severities and different "mass" (one VAL precondition failure vs. ten failing assertions). Collapsing them into one comparable per-(step, constraint) distribution B[s,c] ∈ [0,1] requires a principled severity-weighting and normalization scheme; a naive concatenation or max over raw critic outputs is dominated by whichever critic happens to be loudest, not whichever is most diagnostic.

(b) **Frozen-prefix soundness hazard (the central risk).** KNOB 1 freezes "validated" low-blame segments and regenerates only high-blame cells. But plans are causally chained: an action you froze may have supplied a precondition for a downstream action you regenerated, or the regenerated cell may consume a resource the frozen prefix assumed available. Freezing against a *stale* critic snapshot can silently produce an invalid plan that no longer holds together. Partial regeneration must therefore *re-prove* the frozen segments against the new cells every round — soundness cannot be assumed from the prior validation.

(c) **The localization-vs-regularization confound (why PLACEBO is mandatory).** Partial regeneration *by itself* — simply re-emitting less of the plan — can cut rounds and tokens regardless of *where* blame actually points, because there's less to get wrong and the valid context is preserved. So a naive "CRUX vs full-regeneration" win does **not** prove the localization signal did anything; it could be pure "regenerate-less" regularization. Separating the two requires an entropy-matched random blame field applied with a **single consistent shuffle shared across BOTH knobs** — itself subtle to construct, because an independent per-knob shuffle would leak structure and a non-entropy-matched field would change the regeneration-mask size and confound the comparison.

(d) **Self-attribution circularity.** Non-localizing pass/fail critics fall back to the LLM judging its own blame. An LLM scoring which of its own steps failed is exactly the miscalibrated/Clever-Hans self-evaluation the LLM-Modulo intro warns against — it can rationalize, anchor on surface features, or echo the prompt. Trusting it uncritically reintroduces the unsound self-verification CRUX is supposed to route *around*.

(e) **Credit-assignment non-identifiability.** A single global failure can have many internally-consistent cell-blame explanations — multiple (step, constraint) pairs whose joint violation is observationally equivalent given one failing trace. The estimator must commit to a distribution under genuine ambiguity, and the ORACLE-vs-estimate gap is bounded by this irreducible non-identifiability, not just by estimator quality.

(f) **Cost/benefit must net out positive.** Running constructive critics to localize, plus per-cell re-prompting and frozen-prefix re-validation, adds overhead every round. CRUX only wins if that overhead is repaid by *fewer rounds* and *fewer regenerated tokens*. On short plans with one binding constraint there is little to localize, so the method must be shown to pay for itself precisely where it should — long, multi-constraint plans.

Naive approaches fail because they collapse these: a single "regenerate the whole plan" loop (R1) ignores (a)–(e) entirely; per-critic plan-level pass/fail (R2) has no cell to target; and "trust the LLM's self-diagnosis" ignores (b) and (d) and can degrade soundness.

---

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

*Strand 1 — LLM-Modulo and the planning-critique line (Kambhampati et al., 2024, arXiv:2402.01817; Valmeekam et al. PlanBench, arXiv:2206.10498; Stechly/Valmeekam on self-critiquing plans, arXiv:2310.08118).* This line correctly establishes that LLMs cannot self-verify plans and must be paired with sound external critics, and it makes *constructive* critics a headline feature. But the framework's output interface is a per-critic GLOBAL pass/fail verdict feeding an undifferentiated "your plan failed, regenerate" loop. The localization that VAL and resource checkers compute internally is collapsed at the interface boundary. CRUX changes nothing about the critics' soundness; it promotes that already-computed localization into a per-(step, constraint) blame field B[s,c] and routes on it. LLM-Modulo is recovered exactly as the R2 special case (single global cell).

*Strand 2 — LLM-as-PDDL-frontend-to-solver (LLM+P, Liu et al. 2023, arXiv:2304.11477; LLM-DM / "Leveraging Pre-trained LLMs to Construct & Utilize World Models," Guan et al. 2023, arXiv:2305.14909).* These works sidestep repair entirely by demoting the LLM to a translator that hands a formal problem to a complete solver, so credit assignment is the solver's job and never surfaces. That is a deliberately different design point. CRUX, by the intro's own choice, keeps the LLM *in* the generate-test loop (where solvers don't exist or domains aren't fully formalizable) — which is exactly the regime where blame localization becomes necessary rather than outsourced. The solver-frontend line therefore could not have produced CRUX because it eliminated the problem CRUX solves.

*Strand 3 — iterative self-repair / reflection (Self-Refine, Madaan et al. 2023, arXiv:2303.17651; Reflexion, Shinn et al. 2023, arXiv:2303.11366; Self-Debugging, Chen et al. 2023, arXiv:2304.05128).* These pioneered the repair loop but reflect at the *whole-output* level using feedback that is self-generated (and thus unsound) or undifferentiated. They have no notion of a frozen valid prefix and no mechanism to spend a bounded budget on the worst region. CRUX differs on both substance and mechanism: blame comes from *sound* critics, not self-talk, and it drives *constrained partial* regeneration (freeze validated cells) plus *blame-weighted* budget allocation. Reflexion's "reflect and retry" is essentially CRUX's R1 constant-field degenerate case.

*Strand 4 — STRONGEST PRIOR-ART RISK: step/process-level verification and credit assignment (Lightman et al., "Let's Verify Step by Step," 2023, arXiv:2305.20050; Math-Shepherd, Wang et al. 2023, arXiv:2312.08935; step/outcome reward models, verifier-guided decoding).* This is the line most likely to be mistaken for CRUX, so the boundary must be drawn precisely on four axes at once. (a) **Dimensionality:** PRMs score steps along ONE axis — the correctness of a reasoning step — producing a 1-D vector; CRUX builds a 2-D (step × *constraint*) field, so it can say *which constraint* a step violates, not merely *that* it is wrong. (b) **Source of the signal:** PRMs are *learned scalar reward models* trained on labeled or auto-rollout-estimated step data; CRUX's blame is *read off sound, composable symbolic critics* (VAL cells, resource timelines, failing assertions) and is therefore label-free and verification-grounded rather than a regression target. (c) **Use:** PRMs RANK/SELECT candidates (best-of-n, beam) or supply RL credit to train a policy; CRUX never ranks and never trains — it uses the field to *localize partial regeneration* and to *allocate a finite backprompt-token budget*. (d) **Supervision cost:** process supervision needs expensive labeled step data; CRUX's blame is free as a byproduct of critics already running. So even though both "score steps," they live in different dimensions, draw from different signal sources, and act through different mechanisms. A reviewer collapsing CRUX into "a PRM with an extra axis" misses that the *use* (route + budget, not rank + RL) and the *soundness* (composable verifiers, not a learned scalar) are the load-bearing differences.

*Strand 5 — constrained/partial regeneration, infilling, tool-error localization, and temporal credit assignment (as conceptual framing).* Infilling and constrained decoding can regenerate a masked span, and RL's temporal credit assignment / reward shaping inspire the "distribute blame mass" intuition. But these are *mechanisms and analogies*, not a system that derives the mask from sound verifier output and couples it to a budgeted backprompt. CRUX's contribution is the *coupling*: a blame field that simultaneously parameterizes the regeneration mask (where) and the backprompt allocation (what), with both knobs reading the same field, and a single shared PLACEBO shuffle proving the gains come from blame *structure* rather than mere re-prompting.

*The barrier that kept this unsolved.* The information was never missing — constructive critics have emitted cell-level localization (which precondition, which action, which assertion) since VAL existed. What blocked the field is an *interface* decision: repair loops consumed critics through a plan-level pass/fail verdict, which throws the localization away before it can reach the regenerator. The reflection line had the loop but not sound localization; the PRM line had step-level scoring but used it to rank/train, not to route regeneration; the solver-frontend line removed the loop altogether. No one promoted the discarded verdict into a *routed* (step × constraint) field that both freezes valid work and spends a budget on the worst cells — which is precisely the gap CRUX fills.

---

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

**(1) Blame-field estimator B[s,c].** For each plan with steps s and constraints/critics c, each critic k emits a *localized blame mass* m_k over the cells it can constructively localize, scaled by a severity weight w_k (calibrated per critic type, e.g., a hard VAL precondition violation > a soft resource-slack warning). Concretely: VAL → unit mass on the failing (step, precondition/action) cell(s); resource/timeline checker → mass over the (resource, timestep) cells exceeding capacity, proportional to overrun; unit-test runner → mass on the (step, assertion) cells of failing assertions. Aggregate: B̃[s,c] = Σ_k w_k · m_k[s,c], then normalize to a per-cell distribution (row/column or global softmax-normalize to [0,1], Σ = 1). For non-localizing pass/fail critics, an **LLM self-attribution fallback** proposes a mass over candidate cells, entered at a *down-weighted* w_self and held out as an ablatable source. Output: a dense field B over the (step × constraint) grid.

**(2) KNOB 1 — constrained partial regeneration + frozen-prefix validity guard.** Threshold B at τ to form a regeneration mask: cells with B ≥ τ are "high-blame" and regenerated; cells below are *frozen*. Regenerate only masked cells, conditioned on the frozen context. **Guard:** re-run the constructive critics on the *full reassembled plan*; verify every frozen segment is still valid (preconditions still supplied, resources still feasible) against the regenerated cells. On any broken link → **reject-and-widen**: expand the mask to include the newly-implicated cells (and their causal neighbors) and retry. Report **frozen-prefix VALIDITY rate** = fraction of frozen segments still valid in the accepted plan.

**(3) KNOB 2 — blame-weighted backprompt-budget allocator.** Given a finite backprompt token budget T per round, allocate tokens to cells proportional to B[s,c] (e.g., top-k cells by blame, or water-filling proportional to mass), so the highest-blame (step, constraint) cells get the most corrective guidance; uniform allocation is the R4 reduction.

**(4) Integrated loop.** Generate → run critic bank → estimate B → (KNOB 1) build mask + partial-regenerate with validity guard → (KNOB 2) allocate backprompt budget over residual high-blame cells → re-validate → repeat until a **soundness-verified** plan (all constructive critics pass) or budget exhausted.

**(5) Datasets.** PlanBench (Blocksworld, Logistics, Mystery/obfuscated Blocksworld); classic IPC PDDL (Depots, Rovers, Satellite, Zenotravel); resource/temporal domains for resource-constraint cells; TravelPlanner (Xie et al. 2024) and Natural Plan (Google 2024) for constraint-rich (step, constraint) cells; HumanEval and MBPP+ as a unit-test-critic surface (failing-assertion → step cells) for cross-domain "cell" generality.

**(6) Metrics.** **Primary: mean backprompt rounds-to-sound-plan**, reported only at **ε-non-inferior solve-rate** (DO-NO-HARM floor). Secondary: total token/compute cost to first sound plan; final soundness-verified solve-rate; **blame-field quality** (AUROC/AUPRC of B vs ORACLE cell-blame); **frozen-prefix VALIDITY rate**; regeneration **localization precision/recall**; and a **downstream corpus-quality** metric — fine-tune a model on CRUX-produced sound plans vs. baseline-produced plans and measure pass-rate (tests the intro's data-flywheel claim).

**(7) Baselines + ablations.** Baselines: full-regeneration LLM-Modulo (R1/R3), per-critic plan-level pass/fail (R2), Self-Refine, Reflexion, Self-Debugging (code surface), best-of-n / rejection sampling at **matched compute**, and PLACEBO. Ablations: the **2×2** {KNOB 1 route on/off} × {KNOB 2 backprompt on/off}; **estimator-source** {constructive-only, self-attribution-only, combined}; **threshold τ sweep**; **budget T sweep**; **ORACLE-blame ceiling**. Models span open + closed generators (GPT-4o/4.1-class, Claude-3.5/4-class, Llama-3-70B/405B) to show generator-agnosticism.

**(8) Falsifier ladder.**
- **ORACLE ceiling** — derive cell-blame from the diff of the final correct plan; gives the upper bound on estimator AUROC/AUPRC and the best-case rounds-to-sound-plan. CRUX should sit between PLACEBO and ORACLE.
- **PLACEBO** — entropy-matched random blame field, **one consistent shuffle shared across BOTH knobs**; isolates localization signal from "regenerate-less" regularization. CRUX must beat PLACEBO on rounds-to-sound-plan, else the localization adds nothing.
- **Recovery ladder (each provable-by-construction, unit-tested):** **R1** — constant blame field + mask freezes nothing + uniform budget (all knobs neutral) ⇒ undifferentiated full-plan regeneration; **R2** — single global whole-plan cell ⇒ exact per-critic plan-level pass/fail LLM-Modulo; **R3** — threshold τ→0 (KNOB 1 off) ⇒ full regeneration; **R4** — uniform allocation (KNOB 2 off) ⇒ undifferentiated backprompt; **R5** — KNOB 1 + KNOB 2 both off ⇒ byte-for-byte the full-regeneration baseline. R1/R3/R5 are distinct knob-settings that each provably collapse to the full-regen soundness floor; R2 recovers exact LLM-Modulo; R4 isolates KNOB 1. This guarantees CRUX ⊇ LLM-Modulo (it can never do worse than the baseline in the degenerate limit).
- **LEAKAGE controls** — no ORACLE/final-plan information enters B at run time; obfuscated-Blocksworld (Mystery) guards against memorized plans; held-out instances.
- **DO-NO-HARM** — measured ε-non-inferiority floor on solve-rate; any efficiency claim is reported only where solve-rate stays within ε of full-regeneration. The required, refutable ordering is **PLACEBO < CRUX ≤ ORACLE**.

**(9) Expected outcomes (concrete, directional, falsifiable).**
- CRUX cuts **mean rounds-to-sound-plan by a meaningful margin** (target ≳25–40% on long, multi-constraint instances) vs full-regeneration at ε-non-inferior solve-rate, with a matching reduction in tokens-to-first-sound-plan.
- **Gains scale with plan length × constraint count**: near-zero on short single-constraint Blocksworld tasks, largest on long-horizon Logistics/Rovers and TravelPlanner/Natural Plan instances.
- **Gains vanish toward the R1/R5 limits and toward PLACEBO**, and approach the ORACLE ceiling as estimator AUROC rises — the ordering PLACEBO < CRUX ≤ ORACLE must hold, or the localization hypothesis is refuted.
- **Frozen-prefix VALIDITY rate stays ≈1.0** under the guard (reject-and-widen rarely fires); if it drops, the soundness hazard is real and the guard is doing necessary work.
- **CRUX-produced corpora fine-tune to higher downstream pass-rate** than baseline-produced corpora at equal corpus size, supporting the data-flywheel claim.

---

## Limitations & Responsible-AI Notes

- **Soundness scope.** CRUX inherits — and does not extend — the soundness of the critic bank. The blame field is only as correct as the constraints the critics encode; CRUX re-routes verified failures, it does not invent new verification. Any constraint not captured by a critic is invisible to the field. The "guarantee" is the critics'; CRUX's own claims (efficiency gains, non-inferiority) are **measured**, not proven.
- **Self-attribution caution.** The fallback for non-localizing critics asks the LLM to attribute its own blame — the very self-evaluation the LLM-Modulo intro flags for Clever-Hans/miscalibration risk. It is entered at a down-weighted, ablatable mass and never overrides a sound critic; the estimator-source ablation quantifies how much (if any) signal it safely contributes.
- **Dual-use / efficiency.** Cheaper, more reliable automated planning is broadly beneficial but dual-use; the same loop that plans logistics or code can plan harmful action sequences. CRUX changes loop *efficiency*, not the safety policy — deployment should retain task-level guardrails and human oversight for high-stakes domains.
- **Data-flywheel hygiene.** The synthetic-corpus claim assumes train/eval decontamination and held-out instances (obfuscated-Blocksworld guards memorization); a tighter loop can otherwise amplify benchmark contamination or distributional narrowing. Corpus diversity and leakage audits are part of the protocol; generated-plan diversity will be monitored via n-gram and semantic deduplication before fine-tuning to guard against mode collapse toward the critic bank's easiest satisfying solutions.
- **Scope of evidence.** Named datasets/critics are predominantly English-language and PDDL/code/travel benchmarks; generalization to other languages, open-world domains, and non-formalizable constraints is out of scope for the initial study and an explicit follow-up.
